video_title: A Radical Vision for Israelis and Palestinians | The Ezra Klein Show
gist: Ezra Klein interviews Rula Hardal and Mai Pundak, co-directors of the Israeli-Palestinian 'Land for All' initiative, on their confederation model: two sovereign states (Israel and Palestine) with open borders, decoupled citizenship and residency, shared institutions (human rights court, economy, climate), and mechanisms addressing both Palestinian right of return and Jewish settlement presence. The conversation is less a policy blueprint than a theory of change: they argue separation has failed to deliver security, that a clear endgame (not a road map) is the precondition for progress, and that a bottom-up civil-society movement growing since October 7th is the lever, while Klein repeatedly presses that current Israeli politics and rightward-trending youth make the plan far from feasible.

insights.claim: Land for All proposes a confederation of two sovereign independent states (Israel and Palestine) that rejects the separation/segregation paradigm in favor of a shared layer of joint institutions, positioning it as a distinct 'third option' between classic two-state and one-state models.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Signals a new organizing frame entering the Israeli-Palestinian policy discourse; analysts tracking peace-process narratives should note the confederation model as the emerging alternative that donors and civil-society groups are coalescing around.
insights.quote: yes sovereignity yes nation states yes identity yes borders and there's another layer to that of a shared mechanism of shared institutions

insights.claim: The model is built on two premises: that both peoples have a psychological/social attachment to the entire land 'from the river to the sea,' and that the on-the-ground reality is already intertwined and shared rather than separable.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: The plan's viability rests on reframing shared attachment as an asset rather than a zero-sum liability; watch whether this framing gains traction against the dominant separation logic.
insights.quote: both Israelis and Palestinians have an immense psychological, social connection and sense of belonging to the entire homeland from the river to the sea. That's a fact.

insights.claim: The proposed shared institutions include a joint human rights court plus cooperation mechanisms on economy, climate, public health, water, security, and Jerusalem, framed as issues that cannot be handled by separate states.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific functional domains where cross-border institution-building would begin, useful for assessing which cooperation projects could serve as early proof points.

insights.claim: Pundak argues the classic two-state solution has become 'an empty shell' that political actors invoke to appear engaged while taking no action, a managed-conflict posture she contends led directly to October 7th.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Frames October 7th as a political failure of conflict-management rather than a security lapse, a causal claim that reshapes what 'solutions' should target.
insights.quote: that two-state solution has become an empty shell for people to talk about something, but not take any action. And by any action, we've been led to October 7th.

insights.claim: Klein's own framing opening the episode is that no underlying conditions for a political solution currently exist, that a two-state solution is no longer possible given the number and size of West Bank settlements, and a one-state solution is implausible because neither side will cede self-determination.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: Establishes the interviewer's baseline skepticism, a useful gauge of how a mainstream US commentator now assesses the exhaustion of both legacy frameworks.
insights.quote: I don't see how a two-state solution is still possible given the number and size of Jewish settlements in the West Bank. They're not going away.

insights.claim: Hardal argues a one-state reality already exists on the ground between the Jordan and the sea under a single Israeli power, which she characterizes as occupation, apartheid, and over the last two and a half years ethnic cleansing and genocide.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Captures the Palestinian co-director's framing of the status quo as a de facto one-state system, the analytical starting point for why she abandoned advocacy for a formal equal one-state solution.
insights.quote: we have already a one-state reality or one state construction on the ground between the Jordan and the Sea

insights.claim: Hardal moved away from a one-state solution after concluding that the majority of both Palestinians and Israeli Jews are NOT in a post-national mindset and retain strong ethno-national belonging, making an immediate equal single state unworkable.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: A pointed correction to Western progressive assumptions of post-nationalism; any solution premised on dissolving national identities is, in her research view, mispriced against ground sentiment.
insights.quote: the majority of the Palestinians and the Israeli Jews on the ground in Israel Palestine are not in a postnational mindset the way I thought

insights.claim: The core strategic wager is that the separation-for-security paradigm has empirically failed to deliver safety, evidenced by Gaza, where the billion-dollar wall and maximal segregation preceded the greatest breach.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Directly inverts the dominant Israeli security doctrine; the claim that maximal walls preceded maximal failure is the plan's central persuasion lever toward Israeli audiences.
insights.quote: in the place where we have seen the utmost commitment to segregation and separation... the billiond dollar wall... that is where all hell broke loose that's Gaza

insights.claim: Pundak cites the 1979 Egypt-Israel peace as historical proof that a state once branded an existential threat ('Egypt was considered Hitler') can become the source of Israel's safest border, arguing the narrative can be flipped.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Uses a concrete precedent to argue enemy-to-partner transitions are historically achievable, a template for reframing risk in intractable conflicts.
insights.quote: Egypt after 1973 was considered Hitler. Saddat was considered Hitler... There's a peace agreement and that today ensures my safety.

insights.claim: Pundak offers a mental model that public belief a conflict is solvable rises only AFTER negotiations begin, citing that a month before the Berlin Wall fell and the Good Friday Agreement was signed, people said resolution was impossible.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for assessing tipping-point dynamics: current polling pessimism is treated as a lagging, not leading, indicator of feasibility.
insights.quote: A month before the Berlin Wall fell people said it will never fall. A month before the Good Friday Agreement was signed people say it will never be solved.

insights.claim: Pundak argues the current paradigm has not delivered safety even beyond Gaza, pointing to Israel re-entering Lebanon, tensions with Iran, and instability in the south as evidence that 'there's no place that we actually feel safe right now.'
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Situates the plan against a regional-escalation backdrop; the multi-front insecurity is used as the affirmative case that the status quo is failing on its own security terms.
insights.quote: What about what's happening now with Israel re-entering Lebanon? What's happening with Iran? What's happening in the south?

insights.claim: The plan's signature mechanism decouples nationality from geography: a person votes for their national government (Israeli or Palestinian) regardless of which state's territory they reside in, gaining local civil/residency rights in the host state.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: This citizenship-residency split is the technical distinction from one-state models and the structural device meant to preserve national self-determination amid free movement; the central design innovation to watch.
insights.quote: They will continue having their citizenship rights in their national state. Palestinians vote for the Palestinian government... but they can have residency in Israel

insights.claim: The residency mechanism is proposed as the route to resolve Palestinian right of return: refugees would receive citizenship in the state of Palestine but could apply for residency in Israel at their original 1948 places of expulsion.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete technical answer to the most historically deadlocked final-status issue by separating the symbolic/physical return from Israeli citizenship, a formulation not present in prior mainstream plans.
insights.quote: these refugees they will get citizenship in the state of Palestine but they will be able also to apply for residency in Israel

insights.claim: Pundak concedes open borders would be gradual and security-conditioned, modeled on the EU and Northern Ireland/Ireland/UK, shifting border crossing from a collective ethnic/religious question to an individual security screening.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Clarifies that 'open borders' means sophisticated individualized screening over decades, not the immediate abolition of security infrastructure, tempering the plan's most politically toxic phrase.
insights.quote: basing it on an individual question rather than a collective question

insights.claim: Pundak explicitly frames the EU as taking 50-70 years post-bloodshed to reach free movement and accepts a comparable multi-decade horizon, saying 'I have 50 years to wait for peace. I don't have 50 years waiting for what's going on right now to continue.'
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Sets realistic multi-generational time horizons, signaling this is a long-duration project whose backers accept slow compounding rather than near-term settlement.
insights.quote: It took 70 years, 60 years, 50 years to get to an arrangement of freedom of movement. That's okay. I have 50 years to wait for peace.

insights.claim: Pundak notes hundreds of thousands of Palestinian workers already enter Israel daily under managed security arrangements, arguing the economy already depends on cross-border movement that goes unremarked in security debates.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Points to existing labor-market integration as evidence the binary open/closed border framing is already false, a data point undercutting absolutist security objections.

insights.claim: The plan accepts that West Bank/East Jerusalem Jewish settlers are permanent ('these are not going away') and Palestinian right of return simultaneously, treating both long-marginalized interests as core rather than peripheral, though the directors refuse to frame them as a symmetric trade.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: The distinguishing structural move: bringing the two most explosive final-status interests into the center simultaneously rather than deferring them, which is what makes the model 'radical' relative to Oslo-era negotiation.

insights.claim: Pundak insists on a moral asymmetry between refugees and settlers, holding that refugees have a right to their homeland while the settlement enterprise is illegal, immoral, and based on supremacy, but that Jewish attachment to the land is a permanent fact requiring a mechanism for settlers to remain without privileges or control over Palestinians.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Reveals how the plan navigates the legitimacy problem: it normalizes settler presence while denormalizing settler privilege, a distinction critical to whether either constituency could accept it.
insights.quote: the settlement enterprise is an illegal and immoral enterprise... but with no no privileges control you know terror to Palestinians

insights.claim: Klein observes the plan reintroduces two interests (right of return and settlements) that Israeli governments and negotiators have functionally refused to discuss for decades, arguing their exclusion produced 'disastrous results.'
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: Frames the marginalization of these interests as a causal driver of prior failures, supporting the plan's bet that centering them is a prerequisite for any durable outcome.

insights.claim: Both directors argue any viable solution must abandon the Oslo-era liberal-elite-secular model because both societies are becoming more religious and traditional and less liberal, with the plan grounding itself in religious/historical/cultural identity rather than only international law.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: A structural lesson-learned: solutions that ignore rising religiosity are treated as dead on arrival; the plan's deliberate inclusion of religious constituencies is a strategic differentiator.
insights.quote: Israel and Palestine today are becoming more and more religious if anything more and more traditional less and less liberal both societies.

insights.claim: Pundak reports that religious Israelis attend Land for All events and say it is the first time they feel included in the peace camp, framing outreach to religious constituencies as a deliberate and reassuring departure from the excluding secular-left peace tradition.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Suggests the movement is attempting to broaden its coalition beyond the secular left, a necessary but unproven condition for scaling political support.
insights.quote: this is the first time we feel part of the peace camp. We don't feel that you've excluded us.

insights.claim: The plan requires 'transformative national narratives' on both sides, Palestinians acknowledging the collective history and fears of the Jewish people, and Israeli Jews moving from denying the Nakba to acknowledging it, as a precondition to addressing right of return.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Identifies narrative reconciliation as a gating requirement, meaning the plan is as much a psychological/transitional-justice project as a political one, raising execution difficulty.

insights.claim: Klein presses that the EU precedent breaks down because European peace rested on an agreed post-WWII narrative (Germany was defeated and wrong), a shared 'winner's history' that will not exist in the Israeli-Palestinian case.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: A sharp analytical critique of the plan's favored analogy; absence of a settled shared narrative is flagged as a structural weakness the directors concede.
insights.quote: one way that Europe as we now think of it was built was on... a very bloodily agreed to description of what had happened and that's not going to be true here.

insights.claim: Hardal explicitly reframes the goal downward from love/reconciliation to coexistence, arguing the two peoples will not love, forget, or forgive, and that the political vision must be implemented gradually to create space for deeper transformation by future generations.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Lowers the emotional bar for success to functional coexistence, a pragmatic recalibration that separates near-term political mechanics from long-horizon reconciliation.
insights.quote: I don't want the Israeli Jews to love the Palestinians and vice versa... we don't need to forget and not to forgive but we need to ensure having another situation that we can at least continue living

insights.claim: The directors' central sequencing thesis is that a clear committed endgame (a sovereign Palestinian state as the START, not the end, of the process) is the precondition for progress, and that Oslo failed precisely because Israel never committed to an end state, leaving room for spoilers.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Inverts the standard incrementalist sequencing; the claim that endgame-first prevents spoiler sabotage is the plan's key process-design bet and its main critique of the Oslo framework.
insights.quote: a Palestinian state has to be the beginning of the process in order to get to a reality where we could actually make peace.

insights.claim: Pundak reports an unnamed 'important regional player' told the directors never to mention a 'road map' again, reinforcing their insistence on presenting an endgame rather than incremental steps.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Signals regional-actor fatigue with incrementalism and validation of the endgame-first approach at a diplomatic level, though the player is unnamed.
insights.quote: do not talk to us about a road map we never want to hear that word again ever

insights.claim: Pundak names public health, economy, and climate as the plausible 'coal and steel community' entry points for gradual cooperation, arguing shared threats like a pandemic ('If you have COVID in Tel Aviv, you will have COVID in Ramallah') make separate management impossible.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific low-conflict functional domains where trust-building could begin, the practical proof-of-concept layer investors in the process would watch first.
insights.quote: If you have COVID in Tel Aviv, you will have CO in Ramala.

insights.claim: Hardal notably refuses Klein's push to endorse partial confidence-building steps on the ground, insisting Palestinians will not accept incremental measures without a dramatic plan and timeline, revealing a tactical divergence in emphasis between the two co-directors.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Highlights an internal tension: even within a shared movement, the Palestinian co-director rejects the incremental proof-in-miniature logic, underscoring how endgame-first is a hard constraint, not a preference.
insights.quote: the Palestinians are not going now to accept or agree for actual partial steps on the ground until... there is a need for something dramatic and people from both sides need to see a plan with a timeline

insights.claim: Klein counters that people will not believe in the endgame until they see it work in miniature, and observes that effective PA-Israel security cooperation has been 'pocketed' by the Israeli government rather than laddered into something bigger, eroding PA legitimacy.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: Flags a real-world failure mode: existing cooperation has produced political costs for the weaker party rather than momentum, a caution for any confidence-building-first theory.
insights.quote: the effectiveness of the cooperation on security... has been sort of pocketed by the Israeli government as opposed to been the basis of something bigger.

themes.theme: Political Feasibility and Theory of Change

insights.claim: Klein repeatedly stresses the plan faces a near-total feasibility gap: Israel is the far stronger actor, current politics have 'very very little room' for the vision, and the coming election pits Netanyahu against Naftali Bennett and Lapid, with Bennett historically to Netanyahu's right on these issues.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: Grounds the assessment in near-term electoral reality; the opposition offering no meaningful divergence on the Palestinian question means no political vehicle for the plan exists in the current cycle.
insights.quote: you would need a wholesale change a wholesale change in the structure of Israeli public opinion and leadership.

insights.claim: Klein identifies rightward generational drift as the plan's core demographic obstacle: young Israelis are to the right of older generations, and Israeli society is moving and changing but not in the plan's direction.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: The most durable structural headwind: if the youngest cohort is the most hardline, time works against the plan absent a discontinuous shift, a key variable for any long-horizon feasibility model.
insights.quote: the young in Israel today are to the right of older generations.

insights.claim: Pundak's theory of change is bottom-up civil-society mobilization modeled on the 3+ years of ground work before the Good Friday Agreement, claiming Land for All met 15,000 Israelis in 2025 (which she equates to half a million Americans proportionally) and is growing exponentially since October 7th.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: The one hard quantitative traction metric offered; whether 15,000 engagements compounds into political weight is the movement's key measurable KPI to track.
insights.quote: we have met in 2025 15,000 Israelis that is equivalent to half a million Americans who have been looking for vision and hope and alternatives

insights.claim: Pundak states the movement Standing Together, described as the largest bottom-up Jewish-Arab grassroots movement in Israel, has committed for its 10th anniversary to presenting a political vision that is Land for All's vision.
insights.speaker: Mai Pundak, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: A concrete coalition endorsement signaling the confederation model is being adopted by adjacent grassroots organizations, an indicator of network effects within the peace movement.
insights.quote: Standing Together... they're committed to presenting a political vision. That political vision is ours

insights.claim: Both directors argue the plan cannot succeed on Israeli-Palestinian agency alone and requires serious external pressure and binding commitments from international actors, tying the sequence to recent state recognitions of Palestine as a first step in the right direction.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: External leverage (recognition, diplomatic/legal/economic pressure) is treated as a necessary input, meaning the plan's trajectory is partly a function of foreign-policy decisions in third countries.
insights.quote: There is no way we can't do this with without serious pressure and without serious commitments of international actors.

insights.claim: Both directors cite that Israel currently controls roughly 65% of Gaza, and Klein frames the mainstream Israeli center's actual position as continued Jewish-Israeli security supremacy with more settlement building and tighter control, not the pursuit of any peace vision.
insights.speaker: host (Ezra Klein)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the direction of the status quo (territorial control expanding) that the plan must reverse, underscoring the gap between the vision and the trend line.
insights.quote: There's going to be more control. Israel controls 65% of Gaza now.

insights.claim: Hardal argues meaningful change also requires top-down change inside Israel driven by external pressure, attributing the depth of dehumanization of Palestinians in Israeli discourse to the nature of settler-colonial societies compounded by separation and years of media propaganda.
insights.speaker: Rula Hardal, co-director Land for All
insights.so_what: Frames domestic Israeli discourse as a structural product requiring outside force to shift, reinforcing that internal grassroots work alone is deemed insufficient by the Palestinian co-director.
